topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet had just finished their final, lingering note, and the reception hall erupted into a wave of cheerful noise. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute a little tighter, my gaze sweeping across the room, cataloging faces with an almost clinical detachment. Then, there he was. Standing near the makeshift bar, laughing with a group of people I vaguely recognized, was Alex. My breath hitched, a tiny, involuntary catch in my chest. He looked… different. Sharper, perhaps, or just wearing a confidence I hadn't seen in years. I forced a smile, a practiced, brittle thing, and started to navigate toward the dance floor, my steps feeling strangely heavy, like I was walking through water. Every glance I threw his way felt like an audit, checking for inconsistencies in the narrative I was constructing about this reunion.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," Sarah chirped, nudging me forward. I managed a nod, my eyes immediately locking onto Alex across the crowded room. He turned, and for a split second, the easy warmth I remembered was replaced by something cool, distant. His smile didn't quite reach his eyes. I felt a sudden tension coil in my shoulders, a tightness spreading through my chest that made me want to retreat. I kept my hands firmly planted on my clutch bag, pretending to be deeply engrossed in a conversation with a distant acquaintance. I watched the way he shifted his weight, the slight hesitation before he offered a greeting. It was a subtle dance, and I was acutely aware that I was observing every move, trying to decipher the unspoken choreography between them.

[story 3]

I found myself leaning against a marble pillar, pretending to examine the floral arrangements, my focus entirely fixed on the entrance. When Alex walked in, the ambient noise of the celebration seemed to recede, replaced by a dull thrumming behind my eyes. He moved with an assured stride, and as he approached, I noticed the way he deliberately slowed his pace just a fraction, as if savoring the moment. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I took a slow sip of my water, my throat suddenly dry. I watched his eyes meet mine across the distance—a flicker, a brief acknowledgment—and the air between us felt suddenly thick, charged with an unreadable history I was desperately trying to ignore. I straightened up too quickly, my movements jerky, and turned abruptly toward the exit, needing distance immediately.
